---
title: "A Principled Workflow for Academic Writing"
subtitle: "From idea organization to polished statistical manuscript"
author: "Jun Yan"
institute: "Department of Statistics, University of Connecticut"
date: ""
aspectratio: 169
bibliography: ../book.bib
csl: apa.csl
format:
  beamer:
    theme: boxes
    slide-level: 2
    navigation: horizontal
    header-includes: |
      \setbeamertemplate{navigation symbols}{}
      \setbeamertemplate{footline}[page number]
      \usefonttheme[onlymath]{serif}
      \usepackage{amsmath, amssymb, booktabs}
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Goal for This Hour

Move from "I have results" to "I have a manuscript readers can follow."

We will focus on:

- manuscript structure
- section expectations
- paragraph-level development
- disciplined citation use
- tense choices by section

## Plan for the Hour

| Time | Activity |
|---:|---|
| 0--10 | Research lifecycle and paper types |
| 10--25 | What each section must accomplish |
| 25--40 | Paragraphs, topic sentences, and citations |
| 40--52 | Tense and style discipline |
| 52--60 | Workflow checklist and discussion |

## A Full Lifecycle View

A statistical research project cycles through:

1. idea generation
2. literature check
3. planning and design
4. analysis or simulation
5. iteration
6. writing, revision, proofreading

Writing is not the last clerical step. It is part of thinking.

## Start with a Doable Question

A workable topic is:

- interesting
- connected to prior literature
- feasible with your time and skills
- supported by data or a simulation design
- narrow enough to answer

Early writing task: state the question in one sentence.

Idea-driven projects start with a question; data-driven projects start with
available data. Both require literature positioning. Old data do not
automatically imply old ideas.

## Write Before You Write

Before drafting prose, write an outline with:

- section headings
- subsection headings
- paragraph claims
- planned tables and figures
- key references

The unit of planning is not the section. It is the paragraph.

## Two Main Statistical Paper Types

Application paper:

- solves or illuminates a domain problem
- may use existing or tailored methods
- judged by scientific relevance and credible analysis

Methods paper:

- proposes a general method
- usually needs theory, simulations, and real-data illustration
- judged by novelty, validity, and usefulness

## Common Structures

Application paper:

- Introduction
- Data description
- Methods
- Results
- Discussion

The central promise: the statistical evidence answers a substantive question.

Methods paper:

- Introduction, method, properties
- simulations, application, discussion

The central promise: the method solves a class of problems better than existing
choices.

## Interaction: Identify the Paper Type

For a manuscript you know, decide:

- Is it primarily application or methods?
- What is the central claim?
- Which section carries the most burden?
- What evidence would convince a skeptical reader?

If the paper type is unclear, the manuscript probably lacks focus.

## Title, Abstract, Keywords

Title:

- predicts content
- contains important keywords
- is concise and discoverable [@hairston2003successful]

Abstract:

- includes the high points
- explains what was done and why it matters
- usually avoids mathematical notation [@marron1999effective]

Keywords should not merely repeat the title.

## Introduction: Three Questions

The introduction should answer:

1. Why does it matter?
2. What has already been done?
3. What is new?

It guides readers from a broad area to the paper's specific contribution
[@jenkins1995write].

## Introduction: A Useful Sequence

Common progression:

1. topic and importance
2. current literature
3. gap or limitation
4. contribution
5. roadmap

Do the literature review early. Do not wait until the paper is nearly finished.

## Data and Methods Sections {.smaller}

Data: give enough detail to judge relevance and credibility.

- data source
- collection process
- sampling frame
- time period
- variables of interest
- why the data answer the question

Methods: start with intuition before notation, then specify:

- observed data
- model
- parameters or estimands
- estimators or algorithms
- uncertainty assessment
- assumptions
- theoretical claims, if any

## Simulation Section: ADEMP

For simulation studies, use ADEMP [@morris2019using]:

- Aims
- Data-generating mechanism
- Estimand or target
- Methods
- Performance measures

The simulation must answer a methodological question, not just produce more
tables.

## Results and Discussion {.smaller}

Results and applications:

- state the main pattern first
- avoid narrating every cell
- connect results to the research question
- separate evidence from interpretation

Statistical evidence must be synthesized into an argument [@miller2015chicago].

Discussion and conclusion:

- return to the research question
- summarize contributions
- explain implications
- acknowledge limitations
- identify future directions

The need posed in the introduction should be answered here [@zeiger2000writing].

## Paragraphs Are Units of Thought

A paragraph should have:

- one controlling idea
- a topic sentence
- evidence or explanation
- a clear connection to the next paragraph

If a double-spaced page has no paragraph break, several thoughts probably ran
together.

## Strong Topic Sentences

Weak:

> There are several things to notice in Table 2.

Stronger:

> The proposed estimator has smaller bias than the competitors when censoring is
> heavy.

The topic sentence should tell readers what to do with the paragraph.

## Paragraph Development Pattern

A useful pattern:

1. claim
2. evidence
3. interpretation
4. transition

Example:

- claim: proposed method is more stable
- evidence: simulation table or figure
- interpretation: why this matters
- transition: limitation or next comparison

## Reader Expectations

Readers do not simply decode sentences. They form expectations.

Help them by placing:

- old information before new information
- topic before comment
- important stress near the end of a sentence

This principle is central in reader-oriented scientific writing
[@gopen1990science; @gopen2004expectations].

## Interaction: Paragraph Diagnosis

For one paragraph in your draft or a sample paper:

- underline the topic sentence
- circle the evidence
- mark the interpretation
- identify the transition

If any piece is missing, revise the paragraph before editing sentences.

## What Citations Should Do {.smaller}

Citations should:

- locate the paper in the literature
- support factual claims
- acknowledge methods or data sources
- distinguish prior work from your contribution
- guide readers to useful context

Do not use citations as a substitute for explanation.

Use textual citation when the author matters:

> Smith (2020) proposed a diagnostic for ...

Use parenthetical citation when the claim matters:

> Several diagnostics have been proposed for this setting (Smith, 2020).

Group citations by idea, not by dumping every related paper into one
parenthesis.

## Literature Review Is Argument

A literature review should not be a chronological list.

Organize by:

- problem
- approach
- limitation
- relation to your contribution

Each cited work should help answer: why this paper, why now?

## Tense Choices by Section {.smaller}

Basic rule [@caplan2019grammar]:

- past for what happened or what you did
- present for general truths and what the paper shows
- future for planned extensions

| Section | Typical Tense |
|---|---|
| Introduction | present for known facts; past for specific studies |
| Methods | present for method definitions; past for implementation |
| Data | past for collection; present for dataset description |
| Results | past for analyses; present for displayed patterns |
| Discussion | present for conclusions; future for directions |

Consistency matters within paragraphs.

## Examples of Tense Discipline

Past:

> We fit the model using 10,000 bootstrap samples.

Present:

> Figure 2 shows that the estimator is stable.

Future:

> Future work will examine high-dimensional extensions.

Do not shift tense accidentally within the same claim.

## Style Checks That Catch Many Problems

During revision:

- identify the verb in every long sentence
- check that "data" is plural when used formally
- define acronyms once
- avoid "significant" unless statistically meaningful
- read aloud for flow
- ask a colleague to mark unclear transitions

## Writing and Revising Are Different

Drafting goal:

- capture ideas
- make the argument visible
- tolerate rough sentences

Revision goal:

- test organization
- sharpen claims
- check evidence
- improve sentences
- polish style and tense

Do not try to perfect sentences while still discovering the argument.

## Revision Order

Revise from large to small:

1. research question and contribution
2. section structure
3. paragraph claims
4. tables and figures
5. citations
6. sentences
7. grammar, tense, punctuation

Sentence polishing too early wastes effort.

## One-Hour Workflow Checklist

For a current manuscript:

1. classify the paper type
2. write the central claim in one sentence
3. outline section purposes
4. write paragraph topic sentences
5. attach evidence to each paragraph
6. audit citations
7. check tense by section

## Closing Principle

A polished manuscript is not produced by polishing alone.

It comes from a disciplined workflow:

- clear question
- appropriate paper structure
- section-level promises
- paragraph-level claims
- evidence connected to interpretation
- careful revision

## Selected References {.allowframebreaks}

::: {#refs}
:::
